facts.task: Final integration: final build check and smoke tests
facts.lesson: Use headless display configurations when running GUI-dependent libraries like Pygame in CI/CD pipelines to prevent initialization errors in environments without graphical interfaces.

facts.task: Final integration: final build check and smoke tests
facts.lesson: Inject state-management dependencies, such as ScoreManager, directly into logic-heavy systems like CollisionSystem to ensure decoupled testing and easier verification of side-effect interactions.

nodes.lesson: Centralize configuration in dedicated modules to decouple environment-specific constants from game logic, preventing magic number proliferation and ensuring consistent parameter injection across decoupled engine components.
Enforce explicit type hinting in configuration files to prevent runtime type mismatch errors when constants are consumed by external systems like UI or physics engines.
Decouple input polling from game logic by mapping raw hardware events to abstract action identifiers before passing them to state managers.
Implement an interface-based event dispatcher to prevent tight coupling between the event loop and individual game systems.
Use Python dataclasses for serialization layers to ensure explicit structure; enforce strict type hinting to prevent silent data corruption during state serialization.
Decouple game entity state from rendering logic by using data-only classes; this facilitates unit testing of core mechanics without initializing graphical environments or complex dependencies.
Decouple raw input events from application logic by mapping Pygame events to an abstraction layer (Action enum); this prevents tight coupling between hardware interfaces and game state.
Implement an event-driven mapping table (dictionary) in InputHandler to avoid complex if-else or match blocks, improving maintainability and readability when scaling input bindings.
Centralize configuration in a dedicated module using TypeDict or Typed constants to prevent magic number proliferation across game logic.
Use Environment Variables for environment-specific settings (e.g., debug mode) while keeping static game constants in local configuration files.
Use Python dataclasses for serialization layers to enforce structured state representation; avoid using unstructured dictionaries for game entities to prevent runtime attribute errors.
Implement explicit type-hinting in entity definitions to ensure static analysis compatibility; use 'typing' module imports to catch interface contract mismatches before runtime.
Encapsulate shared data models in dedicated modules; keep them free of side effects to ensure they remain lightweight and reusable across different system components.
Decouple state logic from render logic in UI managers by passing a surface target to draw methods rather than binding the manager to a global display state.
Expose state via public properties or getters rather than allowing direct mutation of internal counters to maintain strict control over business logic transitions.
Decouple UI rendering logic from game state by passing only the surface and configuration objects to static helper functions.
Define shared visual constants in a central config file to prevent hardcoding magic numbers and ensure consistency across rendering modules.
Validate surface availability before drawing calls to prevent pygame.error exceptions when the display surface is not properly initialized or has been lost.
Pre-convert assets (e.g., Pygame convert_alpha()) during the caching phase to shift performance costs from the render loop to the initialization phase.
Implement a centralized Singleton or Dependency Injection pattern for asset management to prevent redundant disk I/O and memory fragmentation.
Pre-initialize pygame font modules before rendering UI components to avoid 'font not initialized' runtime errors.
Inject surface objects into rendering functions rather than referencing global display variables to keep UI components decoupled and testable.
Wrap font rendering calls in try-except blocks to catch 'pygame.error' when system fonts fail to load, providing fallback text or silent failure.
Decouple state management logic from rendering routines by separating the data storage from the pygame.Surface drawing instructions within the class.
Centralize configuration constants like colors and fonts in a shared config module to ensure consistency across different managers and prevent hardcoded magic values.
Require engine initialization states to be explicitly checked before rendering calls to prevent NullPointerExceptions when game components are accessed before the Pygame display mode is set.
Use LRU caching for game asset managers to prevent memory bloat while minimizing redundant I/O during heavy render loops.
Initialize global resource managers with lazy loading to avoid dependency failures if assets are missing or hardware drivers are unavailable during startup.
Inherit from pygame.sprite.Sprite for all game entities to leverage built-in collision detection and update cycle management in Pygame projects.
Decouple entity state from rendering by implementing a data-class or dedicated state object; this simplifies serialization and debug inspection.
Avoid hardcoded resource paths in BaseEntity; utilize a ResourceManager pattern to inject assets, ensuring portability and easing unit test mocking.
Inherit from pygame.sprite.Sprite for base game entities to leverage built-in rendering groups and efficient collision detection logic via Sprite.update() and Sprite.draw() methods.
Decouple resource management from entity instantiation by using a dedicated ResourceManager; inject dependencies rather than hardcoding file paths to ensure testability and asset reuse.
Maintain state via dedicated data objects (e.g., EntityState) to ensure separation between entity logic and visual position data, facilitating easier serialization for save-states.

In Pygame, utilize the built-in `self.kill()` method within an entity's `update` logic to handle lifecycle cleanup automatically, preventing memory leaks and unnecessary sprite group processing for off-screen objects.
Encapsulate constant physics parameters like `BULLET_SPEED` in a configuration module to ensure consistency across entity movement logic and facilitate easier game balance adjustments.
Implement self-destruct logic within the entity's update loop to decouple cleanup responsibilities from the main game engine, preventing memory leaks and stale object processing.
Abstract movement physics into constant-driven velocity vectors to ensure consistent frame-rate independent updates across different hardware profiles.
Enforce strict dependency injection for configuration parameters (e.g., speed, bounds) to allow for easier unit testing of entity movement logic without requiring a full engine state.
Encapsulate boundary logic within the entity update method to decouple screen constraints from the main game loop, ensuring consistency across different screen resolutions.
Implement factory-style methods for spawning projectile entities within the parent entity, using cooldown timestamps to prevent fire-rate abuse without blocking the main event thread.
Enforce screen boundary constraints in the update method using clamp logic to prevent entity clipping outside the game viewport.
Implement cooldown management for shooting mechanics using timestamp comparison rather than frame counting to ensure consistent behavior across different hardware refresh rates.
Design base classes to act as strict interfaces by enforcing inheritance, ensuring all entity types maintain uniform update and draw signatures.
Decouple movement logic from state management by using a dedicated update method that consumes delta time, ensuring frame-independent motion across different hardware.
Encapsulate screen boundary checks within entity methods to maintain self-contained game objects, allowing entities to trigger their own removal via signaling rather than external polling.
Encapsulate screen-bound destruction logic within the entity's update method to ensure memory safety and prevent off-screen processing overhead.
Externalize entity configuration variables to a centralized config module rather than hardcoding movement speeds or dimensions within class initializers.
Use pygame.sprite.Group for optimized batch collision detection rather than manual loops, leveraging collide_mask for high-fidelity, pixel-perfect interactions.

Ensure collision callback parameters match the expected API signature exactly; using named arguments like 'dokill' can lead to unexpected behavior if not aligned with the specific pygame version requirements.
Use pygame.sprite.Group for batch entity management to simplify lifecycle handling, collision detection, and rendering updates across disparate entity types like Players, Enemies, and Bullets.
Enforce strict base class inheritance from pygame.sprite.Sprite for all managed objects to ensure compatibility with group-level update() and draw() methods, preventing runtime AttributeErrors.
Use pygame.sprite.Group for batch lifecycle management; it prevents manual iteration overhead and ensures efficient rendering and update cycles across disparate entity types.

Decouple entity management from specific entity implementation by enforcing inheritance from pygame.sprite.Sprite, allowing the manager to treat players, enemies, and bullets as polymorphic members of a collective group.
Use pygame.sprite.groupcollide with 'dokill' flags to efficiently handle bullet-entity destruction and cleanup in a single pass without manual iteration.
Always enable pixel-perfect collision by assigning a mask attribute to your Sprite subclasses; pygame's default bounding box check is often too imprecise for fast-paced action games.
Decouple collision resolution from physics calculation by using observer-like callbacks; this prevents your physics systems from becoming tightly coupled to game-state managers like ScoreManager.
Encapsulate game state updates within a dedicated update() method to decouple frame-rate logic from component-specific state logic.
Initialize system managers before the main loop to prevent null pointer exceptions and ensure resource availability during the first frame.
Decouple game engine logic from systems by using dependency injection; pass InputHandler, EntityManager, and other controllers as constructor arguments to the Game class for easier unit testing.
Ensure the game loop maintains a fixed time step or uses delta time in the update cycle to prevent physics inconsistencies across varying hardware performance.
Validate interface contracts early; define abstract base classes for managers (EntityManager, InputHandler) to ensure the GameEngine can interact with components safely during runtime execution.
Use headless display configurations when running GUI-dependent libraries like Pygame in CI/CD pipelines to prevent initialization errors in environments without graphical interfaces.
Inject state-management dependencies, such as ScoreManager, directly into logic-heavy systems like CollisionSystem to ensure decoupled testing and easier verification of side-effect interactions.
